state.name: Vermont
state.peak_intrusion_season: March through May during spring squirrel, raccoon, and skunk dispersal, summer for bat maternity colonies in attics and barns, and a heavy rodent and squirrel surge from October into early winter as cold weather drives them indoors
state.climate_factor: Vermont's long, cold winters drive rodents, bats, and squirrels indoors aggressively, and the seasonal-occupancy pattern of vacation properties across the state creates concentrated nuisance windows when homes sit empty for months at a time
state.permit_note: Vermont requires a Nuisance Wildlife Control Operator certification for commercial trappers, and migratory birds carry additional state and federal handling restrictions; large game animals fall under direct Vermont Fish and Wildlife Department management rather than the private wildlife removal industry

Addison County.region: west-central Vermont along Lake Champlain and the Green Mountains
Addison County.habitat_type: Champlain Valley dairy farmland to the west, transitioning into the Green Mountains in the eastern half of the county
Addison County.regional_wildlife: the dairy country around Middlebury and Vergennes drives heavy raccoon, skunk, and Norway rat pressure on barns, silos, and feed storage, and woodchucks dig under sheds and farm outbuildings throughout the valley
Addison County.absent_or_rare: black bears and moose live in the eastern mountains but are managed by Vermont Fish and Wildlife rather than private wildlife removal contractors

Bennington County.region: the southwestern corner of Vermont, bordering New York and Massachusetts
Bennington County.nearest_metro: Albany, NY (about 35 miles west of Bennington)
Bennington County.habitat_type: wooded mountains, small historic towns, and the Battenkill valley
Bennington County.regional_wildlife: Manchester-area vacation homes and second residences see heavy bat and rodent pressure during the long off-season stretches when buildings sit unoccupied, and porcupines damage outbuildings, deck boards, and parked vehicles throughout the rural towns
Bennington County.absent_or_rare: black bears and moose are present in the surrounding Green Mountain forests but are handled by Vermont Fish and Wildlife rather than private wildlife removal contractors

Caledonia County.region: the Northeast Kingdom region of northeastern Vermont
Caledonia County.nearest_metro: the region is its own rural hub; Burlington is about 75 miles west
Caledonia County.habitat_type: the Northeast Kingdom's rolling hills, small farms, and large blocks of working forest
Caledonia County.regional_wildlife: older farmhouses and barns throughout the Northeast Kingdom routinely host little brown bat colonies through the summer maternity season, fishers cause chicken-coop and outbuilding losses on rural properties, and beaver dams flood culverts, driveways, and low-lying farmland
Caledonia County.absent_or_rare: black bears and moose are common in the surrounding Northeast Kingdom forests but fall under Vermont Fish and Wildlife management rather than private wildlife removal contractors

Chittenden County.region: northwestern Vermont along Lake Champlain — the most populous county in the state
Chittenden County.nearest_metro: Burlington is itself the largest metro in Vermont; Montreal is about 95 miles north
Chittenden County.habitat_type: the urban Burlington core, dense suburbs, lakeshore neighborhoods, and rural eastern towns
Chittenden County.regional_wildlife: the Burlington-area suburbs see unusually heavy raccoon and skunk pressure for Vermont, Norway rats are persistent in the urban core and along the waterfront, and lakeshore properties along Lake Champlain see frequent bat colony issues in older structures
Chittenden County.absent_or_rare: black bears occasionally pass through the suburban edge and moose are uncommon visitors, but both species are handled by Vermont Fish and Wildlife rather than the private wildlife removal industry

Essex County.region: the far northeastern corner of Vermont, the most remote and least populated county in the state
Essex County.nearest_metro: the region is extremely rural; St. Johnsbury (about 35 miles south) is the nearest population center
natural_features: Silvio O. Conte National Fish and Wildlife Refuge
Essex County.habitat_type: vast working timberlands, beaver wetlands, and a handful of small mill towns — the most heavily forested county in the state
Essex County.regional_wildlife: beavers flood roads, drainage culverts, and low-lying camp properties throughout the county's wetland zones including the Nulhegan Basin, fishers and porcupines cause persistent damage at hunting camps and seasonal cabins, and bat colonies are routine in older agricultural outbuildings
Essex County.absent_or_rare: black bears and moose are abundant throughout this remote forest country, but both fall under Vermont Fish and Wildlife jurisdiction rather than private wildlife removal contractor scope

Franklin County.region: northwestern Vermont along the Canadian border and Lake Champlain's eastern shore
Franklin County.nearest_metro: Burlington (about 30 miles south of St. Albans)
Franklin County.habitat_type: Champlain Valley dairy farms, the Missisquoi delta wetlands, and forested foothills inland
Franklin County.regional_wildlife: dairy operations across the county generate constant raccoon, skunk, and rat pressure at barns, silos, and feed-storage areas, and the Missisquoi delta concentrates beavers and muskrats that damage shoreline farmland and adjacent property
Franklin County.absent_or_rare: black bears live in the forested eastern hills and moose pass through the same uplands, but both are managed by Vermont Fish and Wildlife rather than private wildlife removal contractors

Grand Isle County.region: the chain of islands in the northern part of Lake Champlain, the smallest county in Vermont by area
Grand Isle County.nearest_metro: Burlington (about 25 miles south of South Hero)
Grand Isle County.habitat_type: lake-island farmland, summer homes, and small year-round communities surrounded by Lake Champlain
Grand Isle County.regional_wildlife: lakeshore vacation homes have heavy seasonal raccoon and bat pressure, and Canada goose populations cause persistent damage on docks, shoreline lawns, and waterfront properties through the summer months
Grand Isle County.absent_or_rare: the water barrier limits most forest mammals common on the Vermont mainland, and the black bears and moose found across the rest of the state are absent here — those species in any case fall under Vermont Fish and Wildlife rather than private wildlife removal contractors

Lamoille County.region: north-central Vermont in the Green Mountains, including the Stowe ski region
Lamoille County.habitat_type: ski resort towns, mountain villages, and large blocks of national and state forest
Lamoille County.regional_wildlife: Stowe and the surrounding ski communities see heavy bat and overwintering-rodent activity in vacation homes that sit unused for months between seasons, and raccoons routinely raid dumpsters, screened porches, and unsecured decks at short-term rental properties
Lamoille County.absent_or_rare: black bears and moose are common in the surrounding Mount Mansfield forest but are handled by Vermont Fish and Wildlife rather than private wildlife removal contractors

Orange County.region: east-central Vermont along the Connecticut River and the New Hampshire border
Orange County.nearest_metro: the Lebanon-Hanover area in New Hampshire (just across the river from Bradford)
Orange County.habitat_type: rolling hill farms, river-valley towns, and substantial forest in the uplands
Orange County.regional_wildlife: rural barns and outbuildings throughout the hill towns commonly host bat colonies and raccoon families, woodchucks burrow under sheds and barn foundations across the farmed valley, and fishers cause occasional chicken-coop predation on rural homesteads
Orange County.absent_or_rare: black bears live throughout the surrounding upland forest and moose occur in the higher hills, but both fall under Vermont Fish and Wildlife management rather than private wildlife removal contractor scope

Orleans County.region: northern Vermont along the Canadian border, in the Northeast Kingdom
Orleans County.nearest_metro: the region is rural; St. Johnsbury is about 30 miles south
Orleans County.habitat_type: the Northeast Kingdom's lake country, dairy farms, and surrounding forest
Orleans County.regional_wildlife: Lake Memphremagog and Lake Willoughby shoreline properties have heavy seasonal bat and raccoon pressure in homes left unoccupied through winter, and beaver dams flood farmland, driveways, and rural roads throughout the county
Orleans County.absent_or_rare: black bears and moose are part of everyday rural life in the Northeast Kingdom but are handled by Vermont Fish and Wildlife rather than private wildlife removal contractors

Rutland County.region: west-central Vermont along the New York border, including the Killington ski area
Rutland County.nearest_metro: Albany, NY (about 65 miles southwest) and Burlington (about 70 miles north)
Rutland County.habitat_type: the small urban core of Rutland, surrounding hill farms, ski resort developments, and large blocks of national forest
Rutland County.regional_wildlife: the Killington-area vacation homes see heavy bat and overwintering-rodent activity during the off-season, the Rutland city neighborhoods generate steady urban-edge raccoon, skunk, and Norway rat calls in older residential blocks, and Lake Bomoseen camp properties have recurring seasonal bat issues
Rutland County.absent_or_rare: black bears and moose move through the surrounding Green Mountain forests but are managed by Vermont Fish and Wildlife rather than the private wildlife removal industry

Washington County.region: central Vermont, including the state capital and surrounding hill towns
Washington County.habitat_type: the small urban centers of Montpelier and Barre, surrounding hill farms, and the Mad River and Camel's Hump mountain country
Washington County.regional_wildlife: bat colonies in the older building stock of Montpelier and Barre are a recurring summer management issue across the county's pre-war neighborhoods, and the Mad River Valley vacation homes and ski-area properties at Sugarbush and Mad River Glen see heavy bat and overwintering-rodent activity during the long off-seasons
Washington County.absent_or_rare: black bears and moose live in the surrounding Camel's Hump and Worcester Range country but fall under Vermont Fish and Wildlife management rather than private wildlife removal contractor scope

Windham County.region: southeastern Vermont along the Connecticut River and the Massachusetts border
Windham County.nearest_metro: Brattleboro is its own small hub; Springfield, MA is about 60 miles south
Windham County.habitat_type: river-valley towns, ski resort developments around Mount Snow and Stratton, and surrounding mountain forest
Windham County.regional_wildlife: porcupines are a more visible and destructive nuisance in the interior hill towns than in most other Vermont counties — deck boards, outbuilding siding, and parked vehicles near camp structures are routinely targeted; Mount Snow and Stratton vacation properties see persistent bat intrusions during the off-season, and Brattleboro's older neighborhoods generate heavy urban-edge raccoon and skunk activity
Windham County.absent_or_rare: black bears and moose are present in the surrounding Green Mountain forests but are managed by Vermont Fish and Wildlife rather than private wildlife removal contractors

Windsor County.region: east-central Vermont along the Connecticut River, including the Woodstock and Quechee areas
Windsor County.nearest_metro: the Lebanon-Hanover area in New Hampshire (just across the river from White River Junction)
Windsor County.habitat_type: river-valley towns, the affluent Woodstock and Quechee area, ski resorts at Killington's eastern edge and Okemo, and surrounding mountain forest
Windsor County.regional_wildlife: the Woodstock-area second-home corridor sees heavy bat and rodent activity in seasonally-occupied properties under mature tree canopy, and the Okemo-region resort properties run a similar off-season bat and overwintering-rodent cycle; rural barns throughout Springfield and Weathersfield host raccoon and squirrel intrusions at rates typical of working-farm landscapes
Windsor County.absent_or_rare: black bears and moose are present in the surrounding upland forest but fall under Vermont Fish and Wildlife management rather than private wildlife removal contractor scope
